title: Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2

abridged.content:
Mixed venous saturation - the global oxygen balance signal

Mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO₂) is oxygen saturation of pulmonary artery blood after venous return from the body has mixed. It is measured from a pulmonary artery catheter and reflects the balance between oxygen delivery and oxygen consumption. A usual adult SvO₂ is about 60-80%, with many CTICU clinicians using ≥65-70% as a practical target when the rest of the picture agrees.

Central venous oxygen saturation (ScvO₂) is measured from the SVC/right atrial region via a central venous catheter. It is not identical to SvO₂ because it does not include the same whole-body and coronary sinus mixture. ScvO₂ is often slightly higher, but the relationship varies in shock.

Low SvO₂ usually means extraction is increased because delivery is low, demand is high, or both. High SvO₂ is not automatically reassuring; it can indicate impaired extraction, microcirculatory shunting, severe sepsis, hypothermia, deep sedation, mitochondrial dysfunction, or sampling error. Interpret SvO₂ with CI, Hb, SpO₂, lactate, urine output, temperature, vasoactive dose, and trend.

expanded.content:
Mixed venous saturations - oxygen left after tissue extraction

SvO₂ is the percentage of haemoglobin still saturated with oxygen after blood has passed through systemic tissues and returned to the pulmonary artery. It is called mixed venous because it includes venous blood from the SVC, IVC, and coronary sinus after mixing in the right heart.

SvO₂ is a global measure of oxygen delivery-consumption balance. It does not identify which organ is underperfused and can hide regional ischaemia, but it is very useful in CTICU because cardiac output, haemoglobin, oxygenation, temperature, shivering, sedation, pain, and vasoactive support change rapidly.

Physiology

Arterial blood leaves the lungs with high saturation. Tissues extract oxygen according to metabolic need and delivery. Blood returning to the right heart has lower saturation. If delivery is generous relative to demand, extraction is lower and SvO₂ remains higher. If delivery is inadequate or demand is high, extraction increases and SvO₂ falls.

SvO₂ is shaped by four major variables: cardiac output, haemoglobin, arterial oxygen saturation, and oxygen consumption. Low CO, low Hb, low SaO₂, or high VO₂ can all lower SvO₂.

Normal range and target

Usual adult SvO₂ is about 60-80%. A practical CTICU target is often ≥65-70%, but it must agree with lactate, CI, urine output, and clinical perfusion. A value of 68% is not reassuring if lactate is rising and urine output is falling. A fall from 75% to 58% may be highly significant.

ScvO₂ is often >70%, but it is not equivalent to SvO₂. It samples upper body venous return and does not fully include lower body or coronary sinus contributions. In shock, regional blood flow changes make ScvO₂-SvO₂ relationships unpredictable.

Low SvO₂

Low SvO₂ means more oxygen has been extracted. Causes include low cardiac output, anaemia, hypoxaemia, fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, sepsis, and increased work of breathing.

CauseMechanismBedside cluesCorrection focus
Low COLess O₂ delivered per minuteLow CI, cool, narrow pulse pressure, oliguria, lactateFix preload, rhythm, contractility, afterload, obstruction
AnaemiaLower oxygen contentLow Hb, bleeding, high extractionAssess bleeding/transfusion need
HypoxaemiaLower SaO₂/CaO₂Low SpO₂/PaO₂Improve oxygenation/ventilation
Fever/shiveringHigher VO₂Temperature, shivering, tachycardiaTreat demand
Work of breathingRespiratory muscles use more O₂Tachypnoea, accessory musclesIncrease support

High SvO₂

High SvO₂ may mean good delivery and low demand, such as a sedated, cooled, well-perfused patient. It may also be pathological if tissues cannot extract or use oxygen. Severe sepsis, distributive shock, microcirculatory shunting, mitochondrial dysfunction, hypothermia, deep sedation, cyanide/toxin physiology, severe liver disease, or sampling error can produce high venous saturation with tissue hypoxia.

High SvO₂ with rising lactate is concerning. It suggests impaired extraction, microcirculatory failure, shunt, beta-adrenergic lactate, liver clearance issues, or technical error. Do not call it reassuring unless organ perfusion, lactate, CI, and clinical trajectory support that conclusion.

SvO₂ and lactate

Low SvO₂ plus rising lactate strongly suggests delivery-demand mismatch. Normal or high SvO₂ plus rising lactate suggests impaired extraction, microcirculatory failure, beta-agonist lactate, liver clearance issues, or another metabolic driver. Falling lactate with stable SvO₂ and improving urine output is generally reassuring.

Adrenaline can increase lactate through beta-2 metabolic effects, but do not dismiss lactate as drug-related if CI is low, SvO₂ is low, urine output is poor, and the patient is cold or mottled.

Sampling problems

Venous saturation can be wrong. A PAC sample must be from the pulmonary artery, not wedged. A CVC sample can be contaminated by infusions or flush. Continuous fibreoptic systems require correct positioning and calibration. If the value does not match the patient, verify source, line position, waveform, port, sampling technique, recent flushes, oxygenation, Hb, temperature, rhythm, sedation, and vasoactive changes.

Clinical patterns

A post-CABG patient with SvO₂ falling from 72% to 55%, rising lactate, narrowing pulse pressure, and oliguria has an oxygen economy problem. Causes include bleeding, tamponade, low cardiac output, graft issue, arrhythmia, anaemia, or increased demand.

A post-valve patient with AF at 145/min, falling SvO₂, low MAP, and lactate rise may have rhythm-driven loss of atrial kick and diastolic filling time. A vasoplegic patient may have low MAP but normal/high SvO₂ if CI is high; if lactate rises, mixed shock or impaired extraction must be considered.

Bedside use

  1. Confirm validity. Check source, waveform, line position, sampling technique, and contamination.

  2. Check delivery. Review CO/CI, Hb, SaO₂, PaO₂, MAP, and perfusion pressure.

  3. Check consumption. Review fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, sepsis, and work of breathing.

  4. Compare lactate and organs. Review lactate, base excess, urine output, skin, mentation if awake, renal/liver function.

  5. Act on the pattern. Low SvO₂ requires search for low delivery or high demand. High SvO₂ with deterioration requires concern for impaired extraction or error.

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - Mixed Venous Saturations

Pearl 1 - SvO₂ is oxygen left over

SvO₂ reflects how much oxygen remains after tissues extract their share. It falls when delivery is low or demand is high.

Pearl 2 - Low SvO₂ is a delivery-demand alarm

Assess CO, Hb, oxygenation, fever, shivering, agitation, pain, work of breathing, and sepsis.

Pearl 3 - High SvO₂ is not automatically good

High SvO₂ with rising lactate may mean impaired extraction, shunting, mitochondrial dysfunction, sepsis, or sampling error.

Pearl 4 - ScvO₂ is not SvO₂

ScvO₂ is easier to obtain but does not fully represent mixed venous blood, especially coronary sinus and lower body contributions.

Pearl 5 - Read SvO₂ with lactate

Low SvO₂ plus rising lactate suggests inadequate DO₂ or high VO₂. High SvO₂ plus rising lactate suggests impaired extraction, microcirculatory failure, beta-adrenergic lactate, clearance issues, or error.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - Mixed Venous Saturations

PAC

A pulmonary artery catheter measures true SvO₂. Confirm PA waveform and avoid wedged sampling.

CVC

A central line can provide ScvO₂. Avoid infusion contamination and recognise it is not identical to SvO₂.

Co-oximetry/ABG analyser

Co-oximetry measures venous saturation and can identify dyshemoglobins if available. Label sample type correctly.

Haemodynamic monitor

Interpret SvO₂ with CI, SVR/PVR, MAP, CVP, PAWP, temperature, vasoactive support, and waveform quality.

Ventilator and temperature equipment

Ventilation affects oxygenation, PaCO₂, work of breathing, and PVR. Temperature affects VO₂ and therefore SvO₂.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - Mixed Venous Saturations

Usual adult SvO₂ is about 60-80%. A practical CTICU target is often ≥65-70% if lactate, urine output, CI, and clinical perfusion agree. ScvO₂ is often >70% but is not equivalent. Low values suggest high extraction from low delivery or high demand. High values with deterioration suggest impaired extraction, shunt, sepsis, hypothermia, deep sedation, mitochondrial dysfunction, or technical error.

tables.heading: Venous saturation values
rows: True mixed venous global oxygen balance
Central estimate; not identical to SvO₂
High extraction from low delivery or high demand
May be adequate delivery/low demand or impaired extraction/shunt/error
Rises when delivery falls or demand rises

rows: CI, Hb, SpO₂, bleeding, rhythm, temperature
Low output, anaemia, hypoxaemia, shivering, pain

quizQuestions.question: Where is true mixed venous oxygen saturation measured?
quizQuestions.explanation: True SvO₂ is sampled from pulmonary artery blood via PAC after systemic venous return has mixed.

quizQuestions.question: Falling SvO₂, rising lactate, oliguria, and narrowing pulse pressure after CABG suggest what?
options: Delivery-demand mismatch requiring urgent haemodynamic assessment
quizQuestions.explanation: The pattern suggests inadequate oxygen delivery or increased demand. Causes include low output, bleeding, tamponade, anaemia, arrhythmia, or high VO₂.

quizQuestions.question: Why is high SvO₂ with rising lactate concerning?
options: It may indicate impaired extraction, shunting, mitochondrial dysfunction, sepsis, or sampling error
It proves tissues use oxygen normally
It only occurs with high Hb
quizQuestions.explanation: High venous oxygen with lactate can mean oxygen is delivered but not extracted or used normally, or the sample is invalid.
